Limit overall carbohydrate intake in adults with diabetes
Avoid fructose- and sucrose-containing beverages
Minimize consumption of foods with added sugar
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · General dietary guidelines

items.title: Importance of Nutritional Management in Diabetes
items.content: Nutritional management (MNT) plays a crucial role in managing both type 1 and type 2 diabetes mellitus. For type 1 DM, MNT aims to minimize weight gain associated with intensive insulin therapy by limiting carbohydrate intake. In contrast, for type 2 DM, the focus is on achieving weight loss through modest caloric reduction, increased physical activity, and a target of at least 5–10% weight loss.
keyPoints: Type 1 DM: MNT should limit carbohydrate intake to minimize weight gain from intensive insulin therapy.
Type 2 DM: Emphasize modest caloric reduction, increased physical activity, and aim for 5–10% weight loss.
Weight loss and exercise independently improve insulin sensitivity.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · Other components (part 1)

items.title: Managing Diabetes During Fasting
items.content: Fasting for religious reasons, such as during Ramadan, poses challenges for diabetes management. Patients should be risk-stratified based on a pre-Ramadan assessment to determine if fasting is safe. Education and regular glucose monitoring are essential for those who can fast safely.
keyPoints: Risk stratification is necessary before fasting during Ramadan.
Patient education and regular glucose monitoring are critical during fasting.
Fasting may not be advised for individuals with high-risk diabetes profiles.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · Other components (part 1)

items.title: Exercise Guidelines for Diabetes Management
items.content: Physical activity is beneficial for reducing cardiovascular risk and improving insulin sensitivity in both type 1 and type 2 DM. The ADA recommends at least 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic exercise, distributed over at least three days with no more than two consecutive days without exercise.
keyPoints: ADA recommends 150 min/week of moderate aerobic activity for individuals with diabetes.
Exercise improves insulin sensitivity and reduces cardiovascular risk.
Avoid vigorous exercise in untreated proliferative retinopathy due to risks like vitreous hemorrhage.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · Other components (part 1)

items.subtopic: Other components (part 2) | MONITORING THE LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL (part 1)
items.title: Importance of Recognizing Psychological Factors in Diabetes Management
items.content: Depression, anxiety, and diabetes distress are significant psychological factors that can affect glycemic control in individuals with diabetes. These conditions may lead to non-adherence to dietary, exercise, or therapeutic regimens.
keyPoints: Diabetes distress is defined by the ADA as negative psychological reactions related to managing a chronic disease like diabetes.
Emotional stress can alter behavior, leading to poor adherence to management plans.
Eating disorders are more common in individuals with type 1 or type 2 DM.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · Other components (part 2) | MONITORING THE LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL (part 1)

items.subtopic: Other components (part 2) | MONITORING THE LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL (part 1)
items.title: Role of CGM and BGM in Monitoring Glycemic Control
items.content: Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM) and Blood Glucose Monitoring (BGM) are essential tools for assessing short-term glycemic control. CGM provides real-time data, while HbA1c reflects long-term control.
keyPoints: CGM detects interstitial glucose, which may lag during rapid blood glucose changes or exercise.
Glucose sensors in CGM devices need replacement every 10–14 days or 6–12 months depending on the device type.
CGM provides data every 5 minutes and includes metrics like TIR (time in range) and GMI, which correlates with HbA1c.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · Other components (part 2) | MONITORING THE LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL (part 1)

items.subtopic: Other components (part 2) | MONITORING THE LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL (part 1)
items.title: CGM Use in Type 1 Diabetes to Prevent Hypoglycemia
items.content: CGM is particularly beneficial for individuals with type 1 diabetes, especially those experiencing hypoglycemia unawareness. It helps reduce the frequency of serious hypoglycemic events.
keyPoints: Individuals with type 1 DM or type 2 DM on daily insulin should use CGM.
CGM can decrease serious hypoglycemia, particularly nocturnal episodes.
Integration of CGM with insulin pumps allows for automated insulin delivery adjustments.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · Other components (part 2) | MONITORING THE LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL (part 1)

items.subtopic: MONITORING THE LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL (part 2)
items.title: Frequency of Glycemic Monitoring in Type 2 DM
items.content: Individuals with type 2 diabetes mellitus (DM) on oral therapy or lifestyle modifications require less frequent glycemic monitoring. Continuous glucose monitors (CGM) or blood glucose meters (BGM) can be used to measure glucose levels 3–5 times per week.
keyPoints: Type 2 DM patients on oral therapy/lifestyle need less intense monitoring.
Use CGM/BGM for measuring glucose 3–5 times/week.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · MONITORING THE LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL (part 2)

items.subtopic: MONITORING THE LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL (part 2)
items.title: HbA1c as a Measure of Long-Term Glycemic Control
items.content: Glycated hemoglobin (HbA1c) is the standard for assessing long-term glycemic control, reflecting glucose levels over the past 2–3 months. It should be measured during initial evaluation and regularly in comprehensive diabetes care. The ADA recommends HbA1c measurement at least twice per year if goals are met, or more frequently when control is inadequate.
keyPoints: HbA1c reflects glycemic history over 2–3 months due to erythrocyte lifespan (~120 days).
Correlate laboratory standards with DCCT reference assay.
Measure HbA1c at initial evaluation and regularly in care.
ADA recommends biannual measurement if goals are met; more frequent if control is inadequate.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · MONITORING THE LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL (part 2)

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF DIABETES | ☑ ESTABLISHMENT OF TARGET LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL
items.title: Individualized Glycemic Goals in Diabetes Management
items.content: The establishment of glycemic goals should be individualized, taking into account various medical, social, and lifestyle factors. This approach, termed patient-centered care by the ADA, ensures that treatment plans are tailored to each patient's unique circumstances.
keyPoints: Glycemic control is crucial in reducing diabetes-related complications, particularly microvascular ones.
The target HbA1c should be <7% generally, with a more stringent goal of ≤6.5% for some patients.
For individuals with cognitive impairment or reduced hypoglycemia awareness, a higher HbA1c target (<7.5% or 8%) is appropriate.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF DIABETES | ☑ ESTABLISHMENT OF TARGET LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF DIABETES | ☑ ESTABLISHMENT OF TARGET LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL
items.title: Glycemic Targets for Elderly Patients with Diabetes
items.content: In elderly patients, glycemic goals should be individualized based on their overall clinical state. Factors such as cognition and health status influence whether the target HbA1c is similar to younger individuals or focused more on avoiding hypoglycemia.
keyPoints: Approximately one in four individuals over 65 years has diabetes.
For robust elderly patients, a target HbA1c <7.0% may be appropriate.
In those with impaired cognition or limited life span, the focus should shift to avoiding hypoglycemia and severe hyperglycemia.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF DIABETES | ☑ ESTABLISHMENT OF TARGET LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL

items.subtopic: PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF DIABETES | ☑ ESTABLISHMENT OF TARGET LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL
items.title: Impact of Glycemic Control on Cardiovascular Outcomes in Diabetes
items.content: Clinical trials have shown that improved glycemic control reduces microvascular complications and can benefit cardiovascular outcomes, particularly in early-stage type 1 diabetes. However, intense glycemic control should be approached with caution in high-risk populations due to the risk of hypoglycemia.
keyPoints: Improved glycemic control reduces microvascular complications.
Early improved glycemic control in type 1 DM can reduce cardiovascular events decades later.
Intense glycemic control may benefit some type 2 DM patients with ASCVD but should avoid hypoglycemia due to associated risks.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · PHARMACOLOGIC TREATMENT OF DIABETES | ☑ ESTABLISHMENT OF TARGET LEVEL OF GLYCEMIC CONTROL

items.title: Insulin Regimen Mimicking Physiologic Secretion
items.content: The goal in managing type 1 diabetes is to create an insulin regimen that closely replicates natural insulin secretion. This involves administering basal insulin to regulate metabolic processes such as glycogen breakdown, gluconeogenesis, lipolysis, and ketogenesis.
keyPoints: Basal insulin is essential for regulating hepatic and adipose metabolism in type 1 diabetes.
Insulin replacement should match carbohydrate intake and insulin sensitivity.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 1 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 1)

items.title: Intensive Insulin Therapy for Glycemic Control
items.content: Intensive insulin therapy aims to achieve normal or near-normal glycemia in type 1 diabetes. This involves patient education, glucose monitoring, and a variable insulin regimen tailored to carbohydrate intake and physical activity.
keyPoints: Insulin delivery methods include multiple daily injections (MDIs), continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (CSII), sensor-augmented systems, and automated insulin delivery (AID) systems.
Benefits of intensive therapy include reduced acute metabolic and chronic microvascular complications.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 1 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 1)

items.title: Individualized Diabetes Care
items.content: While intensive insulin therapy offers significant benefits, it may not be suitable for all individuals with type 1 diabetes. Some patients prefer intermittent injections combined with continuous glucose monitoring over being continuously connected to an infusion device.
keyPoints: Intensive management is encouraged in newly diagnosed patients and during pregnancy.
Patient preference plays a crucial role in the choice of insulin delivery method.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 1 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 1)

items.title: Insulin Formulations: Types, Concentrations, and Clinical Use
items.content: Insulin preparations are crucial in managing type 1 diabetes mellitus. They vary by concentration (U-100, U-200, U-300, U-500) to suit different patient needs, particularly those with high insulin requirements or severe insulin resistance. Rapid-acting insulins like lispro, aspart, and glulisine are preferred for mealtime coverage due to their rapid absorption and reduced hypoglycemia risk.
keyPoints: Insulin concentrations: U-100 (most common), U-200 (lispro), U-300 (glargine), U-500 (severe insulin resistance).
Rapid-acting insulins (lispro, aspart, glulisine) have rapid absorption and onset of action.
Insulin analogues like lispro have altered amino acids for reduced self-aggregation.
Long-acting insulins (glargine, degludec) provide basal coverage with less nocturnal hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 1 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 2)

items.title: Advantages of Insulin Analogues in Type 1 Diabetes Management
items.content: Insulin analogues such as lispro, aspart, and glulisine offer rapid onset and shorter duration, aligning insulin action with postprandial glucose levels. This reduces hypoglycemia risk by matching the decline in plasma glucose after meals. Long-acting insulins like glargine and degludec provide extended basal coverage with modifications that reduce nocturnal hypoglycemia.
keyPoints: Rapid-acting analogues (lispro, aspart, glulisine) have rapid onset and shorter duration.
Insulin glargine has a longer duration (~24 h) and less pronounced peak than NPH insulin.
Insulin degludec offers >42 h duration with reduced nocturnal hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 1 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 2)

items.title: Inhaled Insulin and Long-acting/GLP-1RA Combinations
items.content: Inhaled insulin provides rapid mealtime coverage but requires monitoring of lung function due to risks like bronchospasm. It is unsuitable for individuals with lung disease or smokers. Fixed-dose combinations of long-acting insulin and GLP-1 receptor agonists (e.g., degludec plus liraglutide) offer effective glycemic control with less weight gain.
keyPoints: Inhaled insulin has rapid onset but requires FEV₁ monitoring.
Not suitable for individuals with lung disease or smokers.
Long-acting/GLP-1RA combinations (degludec/liraglutide, glargine/lixisenatide) reduce weight gain.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 1 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 2)

items.title: Optimizing Insulin Regimen in Type 1 Diabetes
items.content: Insulin therapy for type 1 diabetes involves a combination of basal and prandial insulin to mimic endogenous insulin secretion. Long-acting insulins like NPH, glargine, or degludec provide basal coverage, while rapid-acting analogues such as insulin aspart, glulisine, or lispro are used for mealtime control. The timing of administration is crucial: rapid-acting insulins should be injected just before meals (within 10 minutes) or after in cases like gastroparesis.
keyPoints: Basal insulin covers 30–50% of daily needs, with the remainder as prandial insulin.
Individuals require 0.4–1.0 units/kg per day of insulin.
Rapid-acting insulins are adjusted based on preprandial glucose and anticipated carbohydrate intake using an insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio (commonly 1 unit/10–15 g).
Correction factor for high preprandial glucose is calculated as 1 unit per 1.6–3.3 mmol/L over target, estimated from 1500/[total daily insulin dose].
Continuous Glucose Monitoring (CGM) or frequent Blood Glucose Monitoring (BGM) is essential.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 1 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 3)

items.title: Flexibility and Precision in Type 1 Diabetes Management
items.content: Multiple Daily Injections (MDI) regimens provide flexibility by allowing adjustments based on CGM or BGM results, anticipated food intake, and physical activity. This approach aims for near normoglycemia with a combination of basal insulin (e.g., glargine or degludec) and preprandial rapid-acting insulin (e.g., lispro, glulisine, or aspart). The dose is tailored using individualized algorithms that consider fasting glucose levels and mealtime carbohydrate intake.
keyPoints: MDI regimens combine basal and bolus insulin for flexible management.
Basal insulin dose adjusted based on fasting glucose.
Preprandial rapid-acting insulin dose calculated with an insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio (1 unit/10–15 g) and correction factor.
Insulin sensitivity varies; doses must be individualized.
CGM or BGM is crucial for effective management.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 1 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 3)

items.title: Insulin Delivery Devices: Benefits and Challenges
items.content: Automated Insulin Delivery (AID) systems are preferred for type 1 diabetes management due to their ability to deliver precise insulin doses based on algorithms that consider preprandial glucose levels, carbohydrate intake, and ongoing insulin action. These devices offer customizable basal rates and meal-related bolus adjustments, enhancing glycemic control.
keyPoints: AID systems can program multiple basal infusion rates for different times of the day or during exercise.
They adjust insulin delivery based on meal composition using programmed algorithms.
Potential challenges include infection at the infusion site, obstruction leading to hyperglycemia, and risk of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) if disconnected.
Backup plans with long- and/or rapid-acting insulins are essential for device failure.
Sensor-augmented devices can prevent nocturnal hypoglycemia through threshold or predictive suspension.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 1 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 4)

items.title: Pramlintide: Role and Administration in Type 1 Diabetes
items.content: Pramlintide, an amylin analogue, reduces postprandial glucose excursions by slowing gastric emptying and suppressing glucagon. It is used alongside insulin to improve glycemic control in type 1 diabetes.
keyPoints: Approved for use with insulin-treated patients with type 1 or type 2 DM.
Starts at a 15-µg subcutaneous injection before meals, titrated up to 30–60 µg as tolerated.
Reduces HbA$_{1c}$ modestly and dampens meal-related glucose excursions.
Major side effects include nausea and vomiting; dose escalation should be gradual.
Should not be used with other GI motility-slowing drugs or in patients with hypoglycemia unawareness.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 1 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 4)

items.title: GLP-1RAs and SGLT-2 Inhibitors: Considerations in Type 1 Diabetes
items.content: While GLP-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs) and SGLT-2 inhibitors can modestly improve HbA$_{1c}$, their use in type 1 diabetes is limited by increased risk of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), particularly with SGLT-2 inhibitors.
keyPoints: GLP-1RAs and SGLT-2 inhibitors offer modest HbA$_{1c}$ improvements.
SGLT-2 inhibitors are generally not recommended due to increased DKA risk.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 1 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 4)

items.title: Initiation of Type 2 DM Management
items.content: Management of type 2 diabetes mellitus should begin with medical nutrition therapy and an exercise regimen to increase insulin sensitivity and promote weight loss. Pharmacologic approaches include oral glucose-lowering agents, insulin, and other agents that improve glucose control.
keyPoints: Type 2 DM management begins with Medical Nutrition Therapy (MNT) and exercise.
Pharmacologic treatments include oral glucose-lowering agents, insulin, and other agents to improve glucose control.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 1)

items.title: Metformin as First-Line Therapy
items.content: Metformin is a first-line agent for type 2 DM, reducing hepatic glucose production and improving peripheral glucose utilization. It is low cost and can be used in combination with other agents.
keyPoints: Metformin reduces fasting plasma glucose (FPG) and insulin levels.
Promotes modest weight loss and improves the lipid profile.
Initial dose should be low, escalated every 1–2 weeks to a maximum of 2000 mg daily.
Avoid in patients with GFR <30 mL/min, acidosis, unstable CHF, liver disease, or severe hypoxemia.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 1)

items.title: Insulin Secretagogues and Their Use
items.content: Insulin secretagogues stimulate insulin secretion by interacting with the ATP-sensitive potassium channel. They are most effective in individuals with type 2 DM of recent onset who have residual endogenous insulin production.
keyPoints: Effective for type 2 DM of recent onset (<5 years) with residual insulin production.
Sulfonylureas reduce fasting and postprandial glucose, initiated at low doses.
Glimepiride and glipizide preferred over glyburide in the elderly.
Potential hypoglycemia risk, especially in the elderly or with delayed meals/physical activity.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 1)

items.title: GLP-1 Receptor Agonists in Type 2 Diabetes Management
items.content: GLP-1 receptor agonists (GLP-1RAs) are effective for managing type 2 diabetes by enhancing glucose-stimulated insulin secretion, suppressing glucagon, and slowing gastric emptying. They promote weight loss and reduce cardiovascular events without causing hypoglycemia unless combined with agents like sulfonylureas.
keyPoints: GLP-1RAs do not cause hypoglycemia due to glucose-dependent insulin secretion.
Long-acting GLP-1RAs include exenatide, dulaglutide, lixisenatide, and semaglutide, administered weekly.
Higher doses of liraglutide and semaglutide are effective for weight loss.
Liraglutide reduces cardiovascular disease events and diabetic kidney disease rates.
Semaglutide is associated with fewer CVD events but increased retinopathy-related complications.
Dulaglutide reduces both CVD events and microvascular complications, primarily renal.
GLP-1RAs should start at a low dose to minimize nausea.
Black box warning for thyroid C-cell tumors in rodents; contraindicated in medullary carcinoma of the thyroid or multiple endocrine neoplasia.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 2)

items.title: Tirzepatide and Dual Agonism for Enhanced Weight Loss
items.content: Tirzepatide, a dual agonist at the GIPR and GLP-1R, offers greater weight loss than GLP-1RA alone by enhancing incretin effects. It is part of ongoing research into dual and triple-acting molecules.
keyPoints: Tirzepatide promotes greater weight loss compared to GLP-1RA alone.
Dual agonism at GIPR and GLP-1R enhances the incretin effect.
Additional dual-acting and triple-acting molecules are in development.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 2)

items.title: DPP-4 Inhibitors for Type 2 Diabetes
items.content: DPP-4 inhibitors enhance the incretin effect by inhibiting degradation of native GLP-1 and GIP, promoting insulin secretion without hypoglycemia or weight gain. They are used alone or in combination with other oral agents.
keyPoints: DPP-4 inhibitors enhance the incretin effect by preventing degradation of GLP-1 and GIP.
They promote insulin secretion preferentially affecting postprandial blood glucose.
Used alone or in combination with other oral agents for type 2 diabetes.
Reduced doses are recommended for patients with renal insufficiency.
Potential side effects include allergy, hypersensitivity reactions, and severe joint pain.
Evidence suggests a potentially increased risk of acute pancreatitis; avoid in patients with pancreatic disease.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 2)

items.title: Mechanism and Effects of SGLT-2 Inhibitors
items.content: SGLT-2 inhibitors lower blood glucose by inhibiting the sodium-glucose co-transporter in the kidney, leading to increased urinary glucose excretion. This mechanism is insulin-independent.
keyPoints: Inhibition occurs in the proximal convoluted tubule of the kidney.
Reduces renal threshold for glucose excretion, causing increased urinary glucose loss.
Associated with a 3- to 6-mmHg reduction in systolic blood pressure due to impaired sodium reabsorption.
Can lead to modest weight reduction and diuretic effects.
Increased risk of urinary and genital mycotic infections.
Potential for euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) during illness or stress.
Not recommended for type 1 DM or pancreatogenic forms with insulin deficiency.
Empagliflozin and canagliflozin reduce ASCVD events and all-cause cardiovascular mortality in patients with established ASCVD.
May reduce hospitalization for CHF.
Should not be initiated in stage 3b CKD (eGFR <45 mL/min per 1.73 m²) or used in stage 4 CKD (eGFR <30 mL/min per 1.73 m²).
Possible increased risk of bladder cancer with dapagliflozin.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 3)

items.title: Thiazolidinediones and Their Impact on Insulin Resistance
items.content: Thiazolidinediones reduce insulin resistance by activating PPAR-γ, promoting adipocyte differentiation and reducing hepatic fat accumulation.
keyPoints: Bind to the peroxisome proliferator-activated receptor γ (PPAR-γ).
Promote redistribution of fat from central to peripheral locations.
Circulating insulin levels decrease with use, indicating reduced insulin resistance.
Rosiglitazone slightly raises LDL, HDL, and triglycerides; pioglitazone raises HDL more significantly while lowering triglycerides.
Associated with weight gain (2–3 kg), small reduction in hematocrit, and mild increase in plasma volume.
Peripheral edema and CHF are more common side effects.
Contraindicated in patients with hepatic insufficiency or class III/IV CHF.
Rare cases of worsening diabetic macular edema reported.
Increased risk of fractures noted in postmenopausal women.
Induce ovulation in premenopausal women with polycystic ovary syndrome; caution advised regarding pregnancy.
Pioglitazone may be associated with an increased risk of bladder cancer.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 3)

items.title: Clinical Considerations for SGLT-2 Inhibitors and Thiazolidinediones
items.content: Both drug classes have specific indications, benefits, and risks that must be considered in the management of type 2 diabetes.
keyPoints: SGLT-2 inhibitors should not be used in patients with type 1 DM or insulin-deficient pancreatogenic DM.
Empagliflozin and canagliflozin beneficial for ASCVD events and cardiovascular mortality reduction.
Thiazolidinediones contraindicated in hepatic insufficiency or severe CHF.
Both drug classes have potential adverse effects, including increased risk of infections (SGLT-2) and fractures (thiazolidinediones).
Monitoring for euglycemic DKA is crucial with SGLT-2 inhibitors during illness.
Pioglitazone may lower the risk for recurrent stroke or myocardial infarction in insulin-resistant individuals without diabetes.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 3)

items.title: α-Glucosidase Inhibitors: Mechanism and Considerations
items.content: α-Glucosidase inhibitors are used to reduce postprandial hyperglycemia by delaying glucose absorption in type 2 diabetes mellitus. They work by inhibiting the enzyme that cleaves oligosaccharides into simple sugars in the intestinal lumen, thus reducing glucose absorption.
keyPoints: Reduce postprandial hyperglycemia by delaying glucose absorption.
Initiate therapy at a low dose with the evening meal and increase over weeks to months.
Major side effects include diarrhea, flatulence, and abdominal distention.
May increase levels of sulfonylureas and incidence of hypoglycemia.
Avoid use in individuals with inflammatory bowel disease, gastroparesis, or serum creatinine >177 μmol/L (2 mg/dL).
Not as potent as other oral agents in lowering HbA₁c but unique in reducing postprandial glucose rise.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 4)

items.title: Insulin Therapy: Indications and Initiation
items.content: Insulin therapy should be considered for initial treatment in type 2 diabetes mellitus, especially in lean individuals or those with severe weight loss, underlying renal or hepatic disease, or during hospitalization. It is often required due to the progressive nature of type 2 DM.
keyPoints: Consider insulin for initial therapy in specific patients (lean, severe weight loss, renal/hepatic disease, hospitalized).
Initiate with a single dose of long-acting insulin (0.1–0.4 U/kg per day) given in the evening or before bedtime.
Bedtime insulin is more effective than morning insulin for fasting hyperglycemia and hepatic glucose production.
Adjust insulin dose by 10–20% increments based on CGM or BGM results.
Use CGM in all individuals taking insulin.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 4)

items.title: Bile Acid-Binding Resins and Bromocriptine: Emerging Therapies
items.content: Colesevelam, a bile acid-binding resin, is approved for type 2 DM treatment due to its role in metabolism signaling. Bromocriptine, a dopamine receptor agonist, has been FDA-approved but its role remains uncertain.
keyPoints: Colesevelam approved for type 2 DM and hypercholesterolemia; exact role in DM not defined.
Bromocriptine (Cycloset) approved by FDA for type 2 DM; role is uncertain.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 4)

items.title: Initial Therapy for Type 2 Diabetes Based on Hyperglycemia Severity
items.content: The choice of initial glucose-lowering therapy in type 2 diabetes should be guided by the level of hyperglycemia and individualized glycemic targets. Mild hyperglycemia (FPG <7.0–11.0 mmol/L [126–199 mg/dL]) may respond to a single oral agent, while moderate hyperglycemia (FPG 11.1–13.9 mmol/L [200–250 mg/dL]) often requires multiple agents or insulin. Severe hyperglycemia (FPG >13.9 mmol/L [250 mg/dL]) may necessitate insulin as initial therapy to reduce glucose toxicity and improve endogenous insulin secretion.
keyPoints: Mild hyperglycemia: FPG <7.0–11.0 mmol/L (126–199 mg/dL) - single oral agent often sufficient
Moderate hyperglycemia: FPG 11.1–13.9 mmol/L (200–250 mg/dL) - usually requires multiple agents or insulin
Severe hyperglycemia: FPG >13.9 mmol/L (250 mg/dL) - insulin as initial therapy to reduce glucose toxicity
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 5)

items.title: Metformin as Initial Therapy for Type 2 Diabetes
items.content: Metformin is recommended as the first-line therapy for type 2 diabetes by several professional societies due to its efficacy, safety profile, and cost-effectiveness. It promotes mild weight loss, lowers insulin levels, and slightly improves lipid profiles. The dose should be titrated based on CGM/BGM results and HbA1c until glycemic targets are met or the maximum dose is reached.
keyPoints: Metformin recommended as initial therapy by ADA/EASD, IDF, AACE
Advantages: promotes weight loss, lowers insulin levels, improves lipid profile slightly
Titrate dose based on CGM/BGM and HbA1c until target or max dose
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 5)

items.title: Combination Therapy for Type 2 Diabetes
items.content: Combination therapy is often required in type 2 diabetes due to its progressive nature. GLP-1RAs and SGLT-2 inhibitors are preferred due to their cardiovascular and renal benefits. Common combinations include metformin with an SGLT-2 inhibitor or GLP-1RA, metformin plus insulin, or a long-acting insulin with a GLP-1RA. The GRADE study showed that adding liraglutide or basal insulin to metformin provides better glycemic control than glimepiride or sitagliptin.
keyPoints: GLP-1RAs and SGLT-2 inhibitors preferred for cardiovascular/renal benefits
Common combinations: metformin + SGLT-2 inhibitor/GLP-1RA, metformin + insulin, long-acting insulin + GLP-1RA
GRADE study: liraglutide or basal insulin with metformin > glimepiride or sitagliptin for glycemic control
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 5)

items.subtopic: TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 6) | OTHER THERAPIES FOR DIABETES
items.title: Insulin Therapy in Type 2 Diabetes Management
items.content: In type 2 diabetes mellitus, insulin therapy becomes necessary when there is a phase of relative insulin deficiency marked by inadequate glycemic control despite the use of one or two oral glucose-lowering agents. Insulin can be used alone or combined with other medications to achieve glycemic targets. Initially, long-acting insulin at bedtime may suffice in combination with metformin. As endogenous insulin production decreases and insulin resistance persists, multiple injections including both long-acting and rapid-acting insulins are needed.
keyPoints: Insulin therapy is indicated when glycemic control is inadequate with one or two oral agents.
A single dose of long-acting insulin at bedtime can be effective initially.
Multiple injections may be necessary as endogenous insulin production falls.
Daily insulin doses can reach 1–2 units/kg per day due to persistent insulin resistance and weight gain.
Weight gain and hypoglycemia are major adverse effects; GLP-1RA addition can mitigate these issues.
Patients on large insulin doses (>200 units/day) may use concentrated insulin forms.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 6) | OTHER THERAPIES FOR DIABETES

items.subtopic: TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 6) | OTHER THERAPIES FOR DIABETES
items.title: Metabolic Surgery in Type 2 Diabetes Management
items.content: Metabolic surgery is a highly effective treatment for obese individuals with type 2 diabetes, often leading to significant improvements or resolution of the condition. It should be considered when hyperglycemia remains uncontrolled despite optimal medical therapy and the patient has a BMI >30 kg/m^2. This procedure should be performed in certified centers experienced in metabolic surgery and nutritional support.
keyPoints: Metabolic surgery is effective for type 2 DM, especially with significant weight loss or resolution of diabetes.
Considered when hyperglycemia persists despite optimal medical therapy and BMI >30 kg/m^2.
Performed ideally in certified centers with expertise in metabolic procedures.
More efficacious than medical management alone according to nonrandomized trials.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TYPE 2 DIABETES MELLITUS (part 6) | OTHER THERAPIES FOR DIABETES

items.subtopic: EMERGING THERAPIES | ADVERSE EFFECTS OF THERAPY FOR DM
items.title: Teplizumab for Delaying Onset of Type 1 Diabetes
items.content: Teplizumab, a humanized monoclonal antibody targeting CD3 on T cells, is FDA-approved to delay the onset of clinical type 1 diabetes in patients aged 8 or older with preclinical disease. This therapy aims to suppress the autoimmune process that destroys beta cells.
keyPoints: FDA-approved for delaying onset of clinical type 1 DM (stage 3) in patients 8 years and older with stage 2 disease
Targets CD3 on T cells
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · EMERGING THERAPIES | ADVERSE EFFECTS OF THERAPY FOR DM

items.subtopic: EMERGING THERAPIES | ADVERSE EFFECTS OF THERAPY FOR DM
items.title: Managing Hypoglycemia Risks in Diabetes Therapy
items.content: Intensive diabetes therapy can lead to serious hypoglycemia, necessitating reassessment of treatment regimens. Severe or recurrent hypoglycemia may require deintensification of insulin therapy.
keyPoints: Hypoglycemia is the most serious complication of DM therapy
Prevention and treatment involve oral glucose or glucagon administered intra-nasally or by injection
Severe, recurrent, or unexplained hypoglycemia warrants reassessment and possible deintensification of insulin therapy
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · EMERGING THERAPIES | ADVERSE EFFECTS OF THERAPY FOR DM

items.subtopic: ACUTE DISORDERS RELATED TO SEVERE HYPERGLYCEMIA
items.title: Recognizing and Differentiating DKA and HHS in Diabetes Management
items.content: In individuals with diabetes, severe hyperglycemia necessitates assessment for clinical stability. It is crucial to determine if the patient has diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) or a hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state (HHS). DKA is characterized by increased ketone concentration and metabolic acidosis, while HHS involves greater hyperglycemia leading to hyperosmolarity and marked dehydration without ketosis or acidosis. Measurement of $\beta$-hydroxybutyrate in the blood is preferred over urine testing for detecting DKA.
keyPoints: DKA presents with increased ketone concentration, metabolic acidosis; HHS involves greater hyperglycemia, hyperosmolarity, marked dehydration without ketosis or acidosis.
Measure $\beta$-hydroxybutyrate in blood when plasma glucose >13.9 mmol/L (250 mg/dL) for DKA assessment.
DKA is more common in type 1 DM; HHS occurs mostly in type 2 DM but can coexist with features of both conditions.
$\beta$-hydroxybutyrate measurement preferred over urine testing for ketones.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · ACUTE DISORDERS RELATED TO SEVERE HYPERGLYCEMIA

items.subtopic: ACUTE DISORDERS RELATED TO SEVERE HYPERGLYCEMIA
items.title: Understanding Ketosis-Prone Diabetes and SGLT-2 Inhibitor-Associated DKA
items.content: DKA can occur at diabetes diagnosis in obese young adults, often of Hispanic or African descent, with laboratory values similar to type 1 DM. These individuals may recover insulin secretory capacity after treatment and remain normoglycemic with diet or oral medication, a condition termed ketosis-prone diabetes. Additionally, DKA can occur with SGLT-2 inhibitor use in type 2 DM due to glucosuria, potentially leading to euglycemic DKA.
keyPoints: Ketosis-prone diabetes occurs at diagnosis in obese young adults of Hispanic or African descent; they recover insulin capacity post-DKA treatment.
DKA can occur with SGLT-2 inhibitor use in type 2 DM, leading to euglycemic DKA due to glucosuria.
The cause of ketosis-prone diabetes is unknown.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · ACUTE DISORDERS RELATED TO SEVERE HYPERGLYCEMIA

items.title: Recognizing Clinical Features of DKA
items.content: Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA) presents with symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, and signs like Kussmaul respirations and fruity breath odor. These features develop over 24 hours and may lead to a diagnosis of type 1 DM or occur in established diabetes.
keyPoints: Nausea, vomiting, and abdominal pain are prominent symptoms.
Kussmaul respirations and fruity breath odor indicate metabolic acidosis.
Cerebral edema is a serious complication, especially in children.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · DIABETIC KETOACIDOSIS

items.title: Pathophysiology of DKA
items.content: DKA results from insulin deficiency and excess counterregulatory hormones like glucagon and cortisol. This imbalance promotes gluconeogenesis, glycogenolysis, and ketone body formation, leading to metabolic acidosis.
keyPoints: Insulin deficiency combined with hormone excess leads to DKA.
Increased free fatty acids and ketone bodies are key markers.
Markers of inflammation (cytokines, C-reactive protein) are elevated.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · DIABETIC KETOACIDOSIS

items.title: Laboratory Diagnosis of DKA
items.content: DKA is diagnosed by hyperglycemia (>13.9 mmol/L), ketosis, and metabolic acidosis (serum bicarbonate <15–18 mmol/L with increased anion gap). Serum sodium may be reduced due to hyperglycemia, requiring correction.
keyPoints: Hyperglycemia >13.9 mmol/L, ketosis, and serum bicarbonate <15-18 mmol/L indicate DKA.
Serum sodium is reduced by 1.6-mmol/L for each 5.6-mmol/L rise in glucose.
$\beta$-hydroxybutyrate is the primary ketone body synthesized.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · DIABETIC KETOACIDOSIS

items.title: Classification and Initial Management of Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
items.content: Diabetic ketoacidosis is classified based on laboratory values and clinical exam into mild, moderate, or severe categories. Initial management includes IV fluid replacement and insulin therapy, along with addressing the precipitating cause.
keyPoints: Mild DKA: pH 7.25–7.3, serum bicarbonate 15–18 meq/L, normal mental status
Moderate DKA: pH 7.0–7.25, serum bicarbonate 10–15 meq/L, mildly reduced mental status
Severe DKA: pH <7.0, serum bicarbonate <10–15 meq/L, reduced mental status, coma
Initial IV fluid replacement with normal saline or lactated Ringer's
Fluid deficit often 3–5 L over the next 24 hours
Switch to 0.45% saline or lactated Ringer's after hemodynamic stability and adequate urine output
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · Diabetic Ketoacidosis

items.title: Insulin Therapy and Monitoring in Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
items.content: Management of DKA includes a bolus of short-acting insulin followed by continuous infusion. Careful monitoring is essential to ensure improvement.
keyPoints: Initial IV insulin bolus: 0.1 units/kg
Continuous and adequate levels of circulating insulin are necessary
Careful monitoring with frequent reassessment
Use a comprehensive flow sheet for vital signs, fluid intake/output, and lab values
Ringer's lactate may lead to more rapid DKA resolution and reduced hyperchloremia
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · Diabetic Ketoacidosis

items.title: Fluid Replacement in DKA
items.content: Initial fluid replacement is crucial for managing DKA. Start with 2–3 L of 0.9% saline or lactated Ringer's over the first 1–3 hours, followed by adjustments based on blood glucose levels.
keyPoints: Start with 2–3 L of 0.9% saline or lactated Ringer's over 1–3 hours (10–20 mL/kg per hour).
Subsequently use 0.45% saline at 250–500 mL/h.
Switch to 5–10% glucose and 0.45% saline or lactated Ringer's at 150–250 mL/h when blood glucose reaches 250 mg/dL (13.9 mmol/L).
For euglycemic DKA, start 5% or 10% dextrose infusion with insulin treatment to prevent hypoglycemia.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TABLE 416-9 Management of Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) (part 1)

items.title: Insulin Administration in DKA
items.content: Administer short-acting regular insulin intravenously to manage blood glucose levels and resolve ketoacidosis. Adjust the rate based on patient response.
keyPoints: Initial IV dose: 0.1 units/kg, then continuous infusion at 0.1 units/kg per hour.
Increase two- to threefold if no response in 2–4 hours.
For mild to moderate DKA, subcutaneous rapid-acting insulin can be used with close monitoring.
Do not administer insulin if initial serum potassium is <3.3 mmol/L; correct potassium first.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TABLE 416-9 Management of Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) (part 1)

items.title: Potassium Replacement in DKA
items.content: Potassium levels must be carefully monitored and managed during DKA treatment to prevent hypokalemia, which can occur due to insulin therapy.
keyPoints: Assess potassium levels frequently; replace if ECG, urine flow, and creatinine are normal.
If K⁺ <3.5 mmol/L, administer 10–20 mmol/L per hour until K⁺ >3.5 mmol/L.
For K⁺ 3.5–5 mmol/L, add 10–20 mmol/L in each liter of IV fluid to maintain serum K⁺ between 4 and 5 mmol/L.
If K⁺ >5.0 mmol/L, start insulin but hold potassium; recheck every 2 hours.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TABLE 416-9 Management of Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) (part 1)

items.subtopic: TABLE 416-9 Management of Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) (part 2) | HYPERGLYCEMIC HYPEROSMOLAR STATE
items.title: Understanding the Mortality and Complications in DKA
items.content: The mortality rate for Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) is low (<1%) but significantly influenced by underlying conditions such as infections, pregnancy, or myocardial infarction. Cerebral edema is a major nonmetabolic complication, particularly in children during resolution of DKA.
keyPoints: Mortality rate of DKA <1%
Related to precipitating events like infection (e.g., pneumonia, COVID-19), pregnancy, end-stage renal disease, myocardial infarction
Complications include venous thrombosis, upper GI bleeding, acute respiratory distress syndrome
Cerebral edema is a major nonmetabolic complication, especially in children
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TABLE 416-9 Management of Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) (part 2) | HYPERGLYCEMIC HYPEROSMOLAR STATE

items.subtopic: TABLE 416-9 Management of Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) (part 2) | HYPERGLYCEMIC HYPEROSMOLAR STATE
items.title: Key Features and Pathophysiology of Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State (HHS)
items.content: HHS typically presents in elderly individuals with type 2 DM, characterized by polyuria, weight loss, dehydration, hyperosmolality, hypotension, tachycardia, and altered mental status. It is precipitated by serious illnesses or conditions affecting water intake.
keyPoints: Common presentation: elderly with type 2 DM
Symptoms include polyuria, weight loss, diminished oral intake, mental confusion, lethargy, coma
Physical signs: profound dehydration, hyperosmolality, hypotension, tachycardia, altered mental status
Absence of nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, Kussmaul respirations
Precipitants include serious illnesses (e.g., myocardial infarction, stroke) and conditions affecting water intake
Pathophysiology: relative insulin deficiency, inadequate fluid intake, hyperglycemia-induced osmotic diuresis
Laboratory findings: plasma glucose >55.5 mmol/L [1000 mg/dL], hyperosmolality >300 mOsm/L, prerenal azotemia
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · TABLE 416-9 Management of Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA) (part 2) | HYPERGLYCEMIC HYPEROSMOLAR STATE

items.title: Fluid Management in HHS: Balancing Rehydration and Neurologic Safety
items.content: In managing Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State (HHS), initial fluid replacement is critical for stabilizing hemodynamic status, typically using 1–3 L of 0.9% normal saline over the first 2–3 hours. The challenge lies in reversing the significant free water deficit accumulated over days to weeks without causing rapid shifts that could worsen neurologic function. Once stability is achieved, hypotonic fluids (initially 0.45% saline, transitioning to 5% dextrose in water) are used to address this deficit over 1–2 days at infusion rates of 200–300 mL/h.
keyPoints: Initial fluid replacement: 1–3 L of 0.9% normal saline over the first 2–3 hours
Free water deficit can be as great as 9–10 L, reversed over 1–2 days with hypotonic fluids at 200–300 mL/h
Use 0.45% saline if serum sodium >150 mmol/L (150 meq/L)
Balance rehydration to avoid rapid shifts that may worsen neurologic function
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State

items.title: Insulin Therapy in HHS: Initial Bolus and Continuous Infusion
items.content: In HHS, insulin therapy begins with an IV bolus of 0.1 unit/kg followed by a continuous infusion at the same rate (0.1 unit/kg per hour). If plasma glucose levels do not decrease as expected, the insulin infusion rate should be doubled. When glucose falls to 11.1–13.9 mmol/L (200–250 mg/dL), add glucose to IV fluids and reduce the insulin infusion rate to 0.02–0.1 unit/kg per hour. Insulin therapy continues until oral intake is resumed, transitioning the patient to a subcutaneous insulin regimen before discharge.
keyPoints: Initial insulin bolus: 0.1 unit/kg
Continuous IV insulin infusion: 0.1 unit/kg per hour
Double infusion rate if glucose does not fall
Add glucose to IV fluids when plasma glucose falls to 11.1–13.9 mmol/L (200–250 mg/dL)
Reduce insulin infusion rate to 0.02–0.1 unit/kg per hour after adding glucose
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar State

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF DIABETES IN A HOSPITAL OR FACILITY (part 1)
items.title: Hyperglycemia as a Predictor of Poor Outcome in Hospitalized Patients
items.content: Hyperglycemia, whether pre-existing or newly developed during hospitalization, is associated with worse outcomes. Factors such as surgery, infection, and general anesthesia can lead to increased insulin resistance and hyperglycemia due to elevated counterregulatory hormones.
keyPoints: Hyperglycemia predicts poor outcome in hospitalized patients.
Factors like surgery and infection increase insulin requirements by raising glucose production and impairing utilization.
Counterregulatory hormones (e.g., cortisol, growth hormone) contribute to transient insulin resistance.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · MANAGEMENT OF DIABETES IN A HOSPITAL OR FACILITY (part 1)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF DIABETES IN A HOSPITAL OR FACILITY (part 1)
items.title: Glycemic Goals for Hospitalized Patients
items.content: Optimal glycemic control in hospitalized patients is crucial, with specific targets varying based on patient condition. Strict blood glucose control can lead to increased mortality and hypoglycemia, especially in ICU settings.
keyPoints: ADA suggests glycemic goals: 7.8–10.0 mmol/L (140–180 mg/dL) for most patients; 6.1–7.8 mmol/L (110–140 mg/dL) for selected patients, avoiding hypoglycemia.
NICE-SUGAR trial found increased mortality and severe hypoglycemia with strict targets of 4.5–6 mmol/L (81–108 mg/dL).
Perioperative target range: 80–180 mg/dL (4.4–10.0 mmol/L).
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · MANAGEMENT OF DIABETES IN A HOSPITAL OR FACILITY (part 1)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF DIABETES IN A HOSPITAL OR FACILITY (part 1)
items.title: Prevention and Management of Hypoglycemia in Hospitalized Patients
items.content: Hypoglycemia is common among hospitalized patients, often due to variable insulin absorption or changes in nutrition. Protocols should be in place to monitor glucose levels frequently and adjust treatment plans accordingly.
keyPoints: Frequent monitoring of plasma glucose is essential during perioperative periods, infections, or fasting.
Adjustments in diabetes regimen and glucose infusion may be necessary to prevent hypoglycemia.
Preventive measures include anticipating changes in insulin requirements due to factors like renal function decline or nutritional interruption.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · MANAGEMENT OF DIABETES IN A HOSPITAL OR FACILITY (part 1)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF DIABETES IN A HOSPITAL OR FACILITY (part 2)
items.title: Insulin Infusion vs. SC Insulin in Hospital Settings
items.content: In a hospital setting, insulin infusion is preferred over subcutaneous (SC) insulin for patients who are critically ill or unstable due to its short half-life and rapid adjustability. This method allows precise control of plasma glucose levels during the perioperative period or when oral intake is not possible. SC insulin may be used for stable patients or brief procedures (<4 hours).
keyPoints: Insulin infusion preferred in ICU or unstable settings due to short half-life (minutes).
SC insulin absorption can be variable; thus, less reliable in critical situations.
For procedures <4 hours, SC insulin may suffice.
Regular insulin used for IV infusions because it is cost-effective and equally effective as analogues.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · MANAGEMENT OF DIABETES IN A HOSPITAL OR FACILITY (part 2)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF DIABETES IN A HOSPITAL OR FACILITY (part 2)
items.title: Inpatient Glucose Management Strategies
items.content: Effective inpatient glucose management requires basal or scheduled SC insulin supplemented by prandial and corrective rapid-acting insulin. Sliding scale methods are inadequate as they do not provide consistent coverage. Insulin doses should be individualized based on patient sensitivity, with correction factors adjusted for body composition (e.g., 1 unit per 2.7 mmol/L over target in thin patients; 2 units per 2.7 mmol/L in obese patients).
keyPoints: Basal insulin supplemented by prandial and corrective rapid-acting insulin is recommended.
Sliding scale methods are inadequate for effective glucose management.
Correction factors: 1 unit/2.7 mmol/L over target for thin, 2 units/2.7 mmol/L for obese patients.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · MANAGEMENT OF DIABETES IN A HOSPITAL OR FACILITY (part 2)

items.subtopic: MANAGEMENT OF DIABETES IN A HOSPITAL OR FACILITY (part 2)
items.title: Management of Type 1 and Type 2 Diabetes in Hospital Settings
items.content: Type 1 DM requires continuous insulin, preferably via IV infusion during prolonged perioperative periods or serious illness. For brief procedures (<4 hours), a reduced SC insulin dose may suffice. In type 2 DM, an insulin infusion or reduced SC long-acting insulin with rapid-acting preprandial insulin is used. Oral glucose-lowering agents are discontinued upon admission due to risks of hypoglycemia and other complications.
keyPoints: Type 1 DM: Continuous insulin via IV infusion (0.5–1.0 units/h) for prolonged periods; reduced SC insulin for brief procedures (<4 hours).
Type 2 DM: Insulin infusion or reduced SC long-acting insulin plus rapid-acting preprandial insulin.
Oral glucose-lowering agents discontinued upon admission due to risks like hypoglycemia and euglycemic DKA.
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · MANAGEMENT OF DIABETES IN A HOSPITAL OR FACILITY (part 2)

items.subtopic: TOTAL PARENTERAL NUTRITION (TPN)/TOTAL ENTERAL NUTRITION (TEN) | GLUCOCORTICOIDS
items.title: Management of Hyperglycemia in TPN/TEN and Glucocorticoid Therapy
items.content: Both TPN/TEN and glucocorticoids can significantly increase insulin requirements. For TPN, IV insulin infusion is preferred for hyperglycemia management, allowing rapid titration with a separate insulin infusion. In TEN, high-protein formulations may help limit hyperglycemia but often require SC rapid-acting insulin before each bolus feeding. Glucocorticoids increase insulin resistance and glucose production, potentially leading to steroid-induced diabetes if FPG exceeds 11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL).
keyPoints: TPN/TEN increases insulin requirements; individuals may become hyperglycemic.
IV insulin infusion is preferred for TPN-related hyperglycemia.
In TEN, use high-protein formulations and SC rapid-acting insulin before bolus feedings (1 unit per 10–15 g of carbohydrate).
Long-acting insulin (0.1–0.2 units/kg/day) is needed for basal coverage in insulin-deficient patients.
Glucocorticoids increase insulin resistance, decrease glucose utilization, and impair insulin secretion.
Steroid-induced diabetes may occur with chronic glucocorticoid use at supraphysiologic doses (>5 mg prednisone equivalent).
FPG >11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL) during glucocorticoid therapy usually requires insulin therapy.

items.subtopic: TOTAL PARENTERAL NUTRITION (TPN)/TOTAL ENTERAL NUTRITION (TEN) | GLUCOCORTICOIDS
items.title: Insulin Management Strategies for TPN/TEN and Glucocorticoid-Induced Hyperglycemia
items.content: For patients on TPN, insulin can be added directly to the solution after determining the total dose. In TEN, SC rapid-acting insulin is administered before bolus feedings. For glucocorticoid-induced hyperglycemia, oral agents may suffice if FPG is near normal; however, insulin therapy is necessary when FPG exceeds 11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL). Morning steroid administration can be managed with short-acting or NPH insulin to control postprandial glucose.
keyPoints: Add a proportion of total insulin dose directly to TPN solution for nutritional insulin coverage.
SC rapid-acting insulin is given before each bolus in TEN (1 unit per 10–15 g carbohydrate).
Long-acting insulin covers basal needs during interruptions or cycling of TPN/TEN.
Glucocorticoids' effects on glucose are dose-related, reversible, and most pronounced postprandially.
Oral agents may control hyperglycemia if FPG is near normal; otherwise, insulin therapy is needed for FPG >11.1 mmol/L (200 mg/dL).
Morning steroids can be managed with short-acting or NPH insulin to control glucose excursions.

items.subtopic: DIABETES MANAGEMENT IN OLDER ADULTS | REPRODUCTIVE ISSUES
items.title: Individualized HbA1c Goals in Older Adults with Diabetes
items.content: In older adults, diabetes management should be tailored based on factors such as biologic age, comorbidities, and functional status. Highly functional older individuals may have similar HbA1c goals to younger people (<7.0–7.5%), while those in long-term care or with cognitive impairment might aim for <8.0–8.5%.
keyPoints: 25% of individuals over 65 years have diabetes
HbA1c goal for highly functional older adults: <7.0–7.5%
HbA1c goal for those with complex/poor health or cognitive impairment: <8.0–8.5%
Avoidance of hypoglycemia is critical due to potential worsening of cognitive impairment or CVD
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · DIABETES MANAGEMENT IN OLDER ADULTS | REPRODUCTIVE ISSUES

items.subtopic: DIABETES MANAGEMENT IN OLDER ADULTS | REPRODUCTIVE ISSUES
items.title: Management and Screening for Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM)
items.content: GDM complicates approximately 7% of pregnancies, with higher incidence in certain racial/ethnic groups. Screening between weeks 24 and 28 is recommended for those not known to have diabetes. Treatment involves medical nutrition therapy and insulin if needed; oral agents like metformin or glyburide show efficacy without toxicity.
keyPoints: GDM complicates ~7% of pregnancies
Increased risk in Black and Hispanic populations
Screening for glucose intolerance recommended between weeks 24 and 28
Treatment involves MNT and insulin; metformin or glyburide are effective without toxicity
items.reference: Diabetes Mellitus Management and Therapies · DIABETES MANAGEMENT IN OLDER ADULTS | REPRODUCTIVE ISSUES

items.subtopic: DIABETES MANAGEMENT IN OLDER ADULTS | REPRODUCTIVE ISSUES
items.title: Preconception Management for Individuals with Known Diabetes Planning Pregnancy
items.content: For individuals with known diabetes planning pregnancy, intensive insulin therapy and near-normalization of HbA1c (<6.5%) are crucial to reduce fetal malformations. Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) and insulin infusion may improve preconception glycemic control.
keyPoints: Intensive insulin therapy and HbA1c <6.5% essential for planning pregnancy
Risk of fetal malformations increased 4–10 times with uncontrolled DM at conception
Use of CGM and insulin infusion can improve preconception glycemic control
